title: Raging Abe Simpson And His Grumbling Gra

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Iconic catchphrase, perfectly character-integrated but routine use

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Oh, jabber jacks! Schoolhouse don't put out spittoons, I ain't responsible.
jokes.punchline: Grampa uses old-timey exclamation and blames the school
jokes.explanation: Great character voice with 'jabber jacks' and the logic that he's not responsible if there's no spittoon

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad, you gotta stop spitting everywhere!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Consarnit! It ain't my fault — they don't teach spittin' control in school no more!

jokes.text: we'd all love to share in your wisdom, experience, yadda, yadda, yadda
jokes.punchline: Dismissive 'yadda yadda yadda' undermines the supposed respect
jokes.explanation: Efficient comedy showing the teacher's actual disinterest despite polite words

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Yadda yadda yadda, you're all graduating, yadda yadda yadda, you're the future leaders of America, yadda yadda yadda, we're very proud of you.

jokes.text: how many of you drove your house to school today?
jokes.setup: Question about houses, followed by driving houses
jokes.punchline: He actually did drive his house (RV) to school
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection with good structure - absurd question that actually has a logical answer

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse's Grandfather
dialogue.line: I drove my house to school once.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: You did?

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse's Grandfather
dialogue.line: Well, it was an RV, but yes.

jokes.text: No, I'm not Superman. I just own an R.V.
jokes.setup: Kids' confused reactions to driving a house
jokes.punchline: Clarifies he's not Superman, just has an RV
jokes.explanation: Nice deadpan delivery with the Superman comparison being oddly specific

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse's grandfather
dialogue.line: I'm not Superman, I just have an RV.

jokes.text: You're livin'in a fool's paradise, Van Houten.
jokes.setup: Van Houten talks about traveling in RV
jokes.punchline: Grampa immediately contradicts with ominous warning
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa character moment - pessimistic and oddly specific about the dangers

dialogue.speaker: Van Houten
dialogue.line: We're going to travel across America in our RV!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I did that once. Worst three weeks of my life.

jokes.text: If you fell down in the shower, that thing'd be your tomb.
jokes.punchline: Specific morbid scenario about shower death
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny and very specific - classic Grampa doom-saying with vivid imagery

jokes.text: No, I'm not Superman. I'm a judge.
jokes.setup: Callbacks to Superman line from Van Houten
jokes.punchline: Another senior uses the same Superman denial format
jokes.explanation: Mild callback humor but feels a bit forced

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: And how do you plead?

dialogue.speaker: Van Houten
dialogue.line: Not guilty. I'm not Superman.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Next case.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: And how do you plead?

dialogue.speaker: Senior Citizen
dialogue.line: Not guilty. I'm not Superman.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Bailiff, remove this man. We have a Superman on our hands.

jokes.text: Why, just this morning I sentenced my 46th man to death. No, 47th.
jokes.punchline: Casual correction of death sentence count
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with perfect casual delivery - the correction makes it even more disturbing

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: I've sentenced 294 men to death.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Actually, 295.

jokes.text: I do have slippers and an oatmeal spoon.
jokes.setup: Grampa comparing himself to the judge
jokes.punchline: His authority symbols are domestic items
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Grampa's delusional sense of authority

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: The judge wears a robe and carries a gavel. I wear a cardigan and carry a spatula!

jokes.text: Now, my story begins in nineteen dickety-two. We had to say 'dickety,' 'cause the kaiser had stolen our word 'twenty.'
jokes.setup: Grampa begins telling a story with odd date
jokes.punchline: Explains 'dickety' because Kaiser stole 'twenty'
jokes.explanation: Legendary Simpsons quote - perfect absurdist logic delivered with complete conviction. Became a cultural reference.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Back in those days, nickels had pictures of bees on 'em. 'Give me five bees for a quarter,' you'd say. Now, where were we? Oh yeah, the important thing was I had an onion tied to my belt, which was the style at the time. I'd just gotten home from Old Man Walton's farm. I was trying to impress some girls, so I opened my jacket and flashed 'em. But suddenly, there was a tremendous flash of light and the Earth stood still. So I had to walk to school, which took thirty-six hours. And of course, the boys kept saying, 'Slow down, Grampa!' But I kept saying, 'Dickety-six! Dickety-six!' Because the Kaiser had stolen the word 'twenty' for the limes.

jokes.text: I chased that rascal to get it back... but gave up after dickety-six miles.
jokes.punchline: Continues using 'dickety' in measurement
jokes.explanation: Excellent follow-up that maintains the absurd logic and escalates the commitment to the bit

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: We had to say 'dickety' 'cause the Kaiser had stolen our word 'twenty'. I chased that rascal all the way to dickety-six.

jokes.text: Dickety? Highly dubious.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's skeptical intellectual response
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment - using formal language to express doubt about obvious nonsense

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Back in my day, we had to walk uphill both ways in the snow, barefoot, with no shoes!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: Grampa, that's physically impossible. You can't walk uphill in both directions simultaneously.

jokes.text: What are you cackling at, fatty? Too much pie. That's your problem.
jokes.punchline: Grampa's mean-spirited and specific insult
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Grampa's defensive hostility when questioned

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Aha! You think that's funny? Well let me tell you something...

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: You're just like your mother - lazy, stupid, and always asking for money!

jokes.text: discuss how I invented the 'terlet.'
jokes.setup: Grampa wants to digress from his story
jokes.punchline: Claims to have invented the 'terlet' (toilet with accent)
jokes.explanation: Great character comedy with the mispronunciation and absurd claim

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Now, where was I? Oh yeah, I invented the terlet.

jokes.text: Stop your snickering! I spent three years on that terlet!
jokes.punchline: Grampa gets defensive about his toilet invention timeline
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that makes the absurd claim even funnier with the time commitment

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: What? I didn't invent the terlet! The terlet was invented in 1775 by... uh... some English guy. I invented the electric nose warmer in 1987!

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: Grampa, you're saying 'terlet.'

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: That's what I said, the terlet! What's wrong with that?

jokes.text: You already put him in a home.
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting putting Grampa in a home
jokes.punchline: Homer points out he's already in one
jokes.explanation: Efficient dark humor that punctures Marge's suggestion with reality

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we should put your father in a home.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: He's already in one.

jokes.text: Maybe it's time we put him in one where he can't get out.
jokes.punchline: Marge escalates to prison-like confinement
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Marge's frustration, darker than her usual character

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's not a house, Marge. It's a home.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, if you don't do the dishes, it's a prison.

jokes.text: Look at Jacques Cousteau and Goldie Hawn.
jokes.punchline: Uses random celebrity pairing as examples
jokes.explanation: Funny random pairing that shows Lisa's tendency toward pretentious references

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: We need to protect our seniors' rights! Look at celebrity couples - they stay together through thick and thin. Like... like Angelina Jolie and Brad Pitt!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: They broke up, sweetie.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: What about Tom Cruise and Katie Holmes?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Divorced.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Okay, fine - Kim Kardashian and Kris Humphries!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That lasted like a month, man.

jokes.text: What about Social Security, bus discounts... medic alert jewelry, Gold Bond powder... pants all the way up to your armpits...
jokes.punchline: Increasingly specific and absurd list of 'senior perks'
jokes.explanation: Good escalating list with specific observations about aging, building to the visual of high pants

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So what kind of perks do old people get?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Well, we get discounts at restaurants, free coffee refills, and early bird specials.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's it?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Oh, and we can wear our pants up to our armpits without anyone questioning it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Plus, you get to fall asleep anywhere — movies, funerals, jury duty.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: And you're allowed to repeat the same story seventeen times in one dinner.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You also get to complain about young people and the price of things.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: And the best part — you can honk your horn at anyone driving slower than five miles per hour!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You can also eat dinner at 4:30 PM and no one thinks you're weird.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Getting old sounds awesome.

jokes.text: That means the fortune's almost mine.
jokes.punchline: Grampa's immediate focus on personal gain
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Grampa's selfishness trumping sympathy

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Who died?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Do I get anything?

jokes.text: until he was replaced by a Moldermatic and died.
jokes.punchline: Casual mention that automation killed him
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about technological unemployment delivered matter-of-factly

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: And so, after thirty years at the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant, Homer Simpson's career came to an end when automation replaced him.

jokes.text: Over my dead body it will! That's exactly the point!
jokes.setup: Grampa using the expression 'over my dead body'
jokes.punchline: Burns points out that's literally the plan
jokes.explanation: Classic wordplay joke with good timing - Burns taking the idiom literally

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Over my dead body!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that's literally the plan.

jokes.text: How long was that?
jokes.setup: Burns asks if Grampa can go 5 seconds without humiliating himself
jokes.punchline: Grampa treats it as a literal challenge to time
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Grampa missing the rhetorical nature of the question

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, do you think you can go five seconds without humiliating yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Five seconds? You're on!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: One... two... three... four... five! I did it!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, well, you've already started by accepting this ridiculous wager.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: But I counted! Fair and square!

jokes.text: Mesmerists, dowsers, Luddites, alienists... Zoroastrians, alphabetizers- Aha! Assassins!
jokes.punchline: Absurd list of occupations leading to assassins
jokes.explanation: Great escalating list with archaic terms building to the punchline, shows Burns' antiquated worldview

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Let's see... Assassins... Assassins... Ah, here we are. 'Assassins.' Hmm, only two in the phone book. I'd better call both of them.

jokes.text: perhaps if we wait, nature will assassinate him for us.
jokes.setup: Smithers questioning need to kill Grampa
jokes.punchline: Suggests letting natural death be the 'assassin'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Smithers line - practical evil with wordplay, using 'assassinate' for natural death

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: But sir, why do we need to kill Grampa? Why not just let nature be the assassin?

jokes.text: Ah, Marion Barry. Is it time for another shipment already?
jokes.punchline: Assumes it's the scandal-ridden DC mayor
jokes.explanation: Good topical reference for the era, implies the assassin does drug deals with corrupt politicians

dialogue.speaker: Fernando
dialogue.line: M.B.? Ah, yes! The scandal-ridden DC mayor!

jokes.text: Ah, Del Monte.
jokes.punchline: Mistakes photo for fruit company logo
jokes.explanation: Visual gag playing on Grampa's appearance resembling corporate imagery

dialogue.speaker: Fernando
dialogue.line: What is this? A fax from Springfield?

dialogue.speaker: Fernando
dialogue.line: Ah, what a beautiful fruit company logo! So elegant, so refined!

dialogue.speaker: Fernando
dialogue.line: I will make it the new symbol of my company!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: That's me!

jokes.text: Enjoy them, old man. They will be your last.
jokes.punchline: Ominous threat about the fruit being his last
jokes.explanation: Standard villain line but fits the fruit confusion setup

dialogue.speaker: Fernando
dialogue.line: Here, Señor Simpson, some fruit for you.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you, Fernando!

dialogue.speaker: Fernando
dialogue.line: Enjoy it... it will be your last.

jokes.text: Ah, he's more clever than he looks.
jokes.punchline: Crediting Grampa with cleverness when it was just luck
jokes.explanation: Mild irony about mistaking luck for intelligence

dialogue.speaker: Fernando
dialogue.line: You missed! How is this possible?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I didn't miss. I was aiming for the other guy.

dialogue.speaker: Fernando
dialogue.line: Brilliant! What a mastermind!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Eh?

jokes.text: There is one more way to kill a man... but it is as intricate and precise... as a well-played game of chess.
jokes.punchline: Next scene shows simple explosive trap
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection between elegant description and crude reality

dialogue.speaker: Fernando
dialogue.line: I would construct an elaborate series of pulleys and counterweights, each precisely calibrated to trigger in sequence. First, a chandelier would fall, then mechanical arms would emerge from the walls, followed by a floor that opens to reveal a pit of—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, but how do you actually kill the guy?

dialogue.speaker: Fernando
dialogue.line: Oh, that's simple. I just put a bomb in a box.

jokes.text: Was that me, or was that you?
jokes.punchline: Grampa's confusion about source
jokes.explanation: Lowbrow elderly humor, character-appropriate but not clever

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Was that me?

jokes.text: Okay, we'll do something about that right away. Let's start by doubling your medication.
jokes.punchline: Nurse treating paranoia with more drugs instead of investigation
jokes.explanation: Dark satire of elderly care dismissing legitimate concerns

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: They're trying to kill me! I've been shot at, poisoned, run over!

dialogue.speaker: Nurse
dialogue.line: I see. Well, let's get you something for that paranoia.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Paranoia?! I'm telling you, it's real!

dialogue.speaker: Nurse
dialogue.line: Of course it is, dear. Here, take these pills.

jokes.text: Sweet merciful McGillicutty! You gotta open the door!
jokes.punchline: Old-timey exclamation 'McGillicutty'
jokes.explanation: Great character-specific exclamation, shows Grampa's old-fashioned speech patterns

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: McGillicutty!

jokes.text: And it sounds like he's gotten into the horseradish again.
jokes.punchline: Homer attributes it to horseradish consumption
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - specific and absurd explanation for Grampa's state

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad, why are you acting so crazy?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I don't know, boy! One minute I'm enjoying a nice sandwich, the next minute I'm seeing purple elephants!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Purple elephants? Did you eat the horseradish again?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Just a little bit!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad, that's the third time this week. You gotta stop eating that horseradish straight from the jar.

jokes.text: And then a knife flew at my head! And you were there... and you were there.
jokes.punchline: Transitions into Wizard of Oz reference
jokes.explanation: Pop culture reference that works with Grampa's confused storytelling

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: So I says to the guy, I says, 'You'll never take me alive, copper!' And he says, 'That can be arranged.' Next thing I know, I'm wakin' up in this poppy field...

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: And there's this good witch tellin' me to follow the yellow brick road. I said, 'Lady, I'm from Springfield, not Kansas!'

jokes.text: Bart's room. Bart's room. Bart's room. Dumpster.
jokes.setup: Family deciding where Grampa should stay
jokes.punchline: Everyone says Bart's room, Bart suggests dumpster
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation with great economy - shows Bart's selfishness contrasted with family consensus

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Grampa needs a place to stay. What about Bart's room?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's a good idea.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I agree.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey! What about the dumpster?

jokes.text: Wrinkly gibberish?
jokes.punchline: Describes the physical document as 'wrinkly gibberish'
jokes.explanation: Good visual description that maintains Bart's skepticism

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That story is gibberish.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: What? My story is a classic!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It's wrinkly gibberish.

jokes.text: I got this in the Second World War II.
jokes.punchline: Calls it 'Second World War II' instead of 'World War II'
jokes.explanation: Classic Grampa malapropism that sounds almost right but is clearly wrong

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: This here's my document from the Second World War II.

jokes.text: the fightingest squad in the fightingest company... in the third-fightingest battalion in the army.
jokes.setup: Grampa describing his unit's fighting prowess
jokes.punchline: Deflating escalation to only third-best battalion
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure building up expectation then deflating it perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: We were the best battalion in the whole army! Well, third best. Okay, we were terrible, but we were the third best at being terrible.

jokes.text: Um, if anybody finds a grenade without a pin, that's mine.
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of dangerous live grenade
jokes.explanation: Great character introduction showing inherited Wiggum incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: I'm Iggy Wiggum. I have a live grenade.

jokes.text: Well, I didn't join the service to make friends.
jokes.punchline: His defensive, antisocial response
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner character moment - uptight and defensive

dialogue.speaker: Skinner
dialogue.line: I've had enough of these pranks! I'm going to my office where I can be alone with my feelings and my stack of unmarked test papers!

jokes.text: Haven't you won the war yet?
jokes.punchline: Dismissive question about war progress
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns entitlement and dismissiveness toward others' efforts

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well? How goes the battle?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Not well, sir. We've lost the north ridge.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: The north ridge? Blast! There goes my pheasant hunting.

jokes.text: Duh, hey! You said you was dead.
jokes.setup: Burns arrives after being thought dead
jokes.punchline: Ox's simple observation about the contradiction
jokes.explanation: Good simple character moment showing Ox's literal-mindedness

dialogue.speaker: Ox
dialogue.line: But Mr. Burns, if you're alive, who did we bury?

jokes.text: Well, he got busted down for obstructing a probe from J. Edgar Hoover.
jokes.setup: Bart asking why Burns was only a private
jokes.punchline: Suggests Burns had FBI troubles
jokes.explanation: Good background gag suggesting Burns' shady history goes way back

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Why was Mr. Burns only a private in the military?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Well, he had some trouble with the FBI. They said he was too dangerous to give a higher rank.

jokes.text: They took a photo of my keister for Stars and Stripes! At least they told me it was for Stars and Stripes.
jokes.punchline: Implies the photo may have been for other purposes
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor escalation suggesting military exploitation

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: This here's a photo of me during the war. I was quite the hero back then.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Dad, that's just you in your underwear flexing in front of the mirror.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but I was a YOUNG man's underwear at the time!

jokes.text: We'll all be rich- rich as Nazis.
jokes.setup: Burns persuading squad to take artwork
jokes.punchline: Comparison to Nazi wealth while stealing from them
jokes.explanation: Dark irony of aspiring to Nazi wealth while fighting them

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Gentlemen, I've decided to add some culture to my collection. That painting is worth a fortune.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: The Nazis had excellent taste in art. Of course, I'm simply borrowing from their... future estate sales.

jokes.text: Wow! I'd like to be as rich as the Nazis!
jokes.punchline: Ox enthusiastically agrees without understanding implications
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable humor from Ox's innocent agreement to terrible sentiment

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: My fortune is so vast, I could buy anything! Why, I'm richer than a Nazi!

dialogue.speaker: Ox
dialogue.line: Yeah! Nazis are great!

jokes.text: I could buy chicken dinners three times a day.
jokes.setup: Discussion of what to do with Nazi wealth
jokes.punchline: Modest Depression-era aspiration
jokes.explanation: Good period-appropriate modest dream showing simpler times

dialogue.speaker: Soldier
dialogue.line: What should we do with all this Nazi wealth?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: I just want enough money to buy a turkey and some beer for Thanksgiving.

dialogue.speaker: Soldier
dialogue.line: That's all? Just a turkey and beer?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, maybe some stuffing too. I'm not greedy.

jokes.text: I'd hate to wind up in one of them old folks' homes.
jokes.setup: Grampa explaining why he needs the money
jokes.punchline: Ironic since he's currently in an old folks' home
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic irony - Grampa's worst fear has come true

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I need the money to get out of this old folks' home!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Dad, you're already in an old folks' home.

jokes.text: Well put, Oxford.
jokes.punchline: Burns sarcastically calling him Oxford
jokes.explanation: Good Burns condescension, ironic since Ox actually understood the concept

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Oxford, is it? How delightfully pretentious. I suppose next you'll be telling me you summer in the Hamptons and winter in Monaco.

jokes.text: Ox was the first to go. He got a hernia carrying the crate out of the castle.
jokes.punchline: Ox died from the very act of stealing the treasure
jokes.explanation: Dark irony of dying from the crime itself, anticlimactic death

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Ox died from the very act of stealing the treasure

jokes.text: Five more men died in the Veterans Day float disaster of'79.
jokes.punchline: Absurd mass death at celebratory event
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - veterans dying at their own celebration, very economical

jokes.text: Could've used a vampire though.
jokes.setup: Bart's dismissive response to Grampa's story
jokes.punchline: Suggests story needs supernatural elements to be interesting
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart response - dismissive of real drama, wants fantasy violence

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Your story sucks, Grampa. You need ghosts or something.

jokes.text: I won't eat much, and I don't know the difference between right and wrong.
jokes.setup: Bart asking to go with Burns for treasure
jokes.punchline: Lists his qualifications as being amoral and low-maintenance
jokes.explanation: Excellent Bart character moment - perfectly captures his amorality as a selling point

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, take me with you! I'm amoral and low-maintenance.

jokes.text: the child labor people have been watching me like a hawk.
jokes.punchline: Implies he regularly exploits children
jokes.explanation: Good Burns character consistency - casual admission of illegal practices

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, want to buy some chocolate?

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: No thank you. I already have a child I'm exploiting.

jokes.text: As they say, 'time is Monets.'
jokes.setup: Burns leaving to get the paintings
jokes.punchline: Art-themed pun on 'time is money'
jokes.explanation: Clever pun that fits Burns' pretentious character and the art theft context

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I'm off to get my paintings appraised. After all, time is money.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, I believe you mean 'time is art.'

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Bah! In my day, time was money, and art was just something poor people did instead of working.

jokes.text: Oh, terribly sorry. Back to sleep, little girl. Santa?
jokes.setup: Burns sneaking through house at night
jokes.punchline: Lisa mistakes the old man for Santa
jokes.explanation: Sweet visual gag with Lisa's innocent mistake about the intruder

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Santa? Is that you?

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Ho ho ho... yes, child, it is I, Santa Claus.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But you smell like cigars and evil.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Ah yes, well... I've been very naughty this year.

jokes.text: You gotta do it for Ox and Asa and Griff and Burnsie! Well, not so much Burnsie.
jokes.setup: Bart motivating Grampa to get treasure
jokes.punchline: Excludes Burns from the honor roll
jokes.explanation: Good callback structure with logical exception for the antagonist

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Come on, Grampa! There's treasure buried somewhere in Springfield!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Treasure? Why didn't you say so! I'm in!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: We just need to find the map and dig it up before Mr. Burns does.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Burns? That cheapskate! He owes me three weeks of back pay from 1952!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So you'll help?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: You bet I will! And when we find that treasure, we'll be on the honor roll!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Um, Grampa, the honor roll is for grades, not treasure.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, it should exclude Burns anyway. That old skinflint never learned nothing!

jokes.text: put your manhood to the ultimate test and take- Pass.
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately opts out
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer cowardice moment with good comic timing

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: We had to cross a bridge made of human bones, dodge poisoned darts, and fight off wild animals with our bare hands!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nope. Not doing it.

jokes.text: If they're anything like me, they have to get up twice.
jokes.setup: Bart asking if dead buddies walk around at night
jokes.punchline: Grampa interprets as bathroom trips
jokes.explanation: Great old age humor with perfect misdirection from supernatural to biological

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Do dead people walk around at night?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Oh sure, all the time. I'm up six times a night myself.

jokes.text: you're all welcome to visit me in Rich Man's Heaven.
jokes.punchline: Assumes separate heaven for the wealthy
jokes.explanation: Good social commentary joke about class divisions extending to afterlife

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, boys, we're all here together again. I wonder where all the rich fellas ended up.

jokes.text: Well, howdy-doodilly, stranger. Couldn't help but notice you stealing my boat.
jokes.setup: Flanders catches someone stealing his boat
jokes.punchline: Cheerful greeting despite the theft
jokes.explanation: Perfect Flanders character moment - aggressively cheerful even when being robbed

dialogue.speaker: Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, hi-diddly-ho, neighbor! I see you're taking my boat for a spin. That's just fine and dandy! Would you like some lemonade when you get back? Okily-dokily!

jokes.text: Ooh, sounds spine-tingly-dingling. Just promise you'll have a good time.
jokes.punchline: Enthusiastically supportive of mysterious criminal activity
jokes.explanation: Great Flanders vocabulary and attitude - supportive to a fault

dialogue.speaker: Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I'd be happy to help with your secret boat mission, no questions asked!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Really? Even though it's probably illegal?

dialogue.speaker: Flanders
dialogue.line: Oh, you betcha! As long as nobody gets hurt, I'm in!

jokes.text: You could've made sergeant.
jokes.punchline: Despite all negative qualities, could still outrank others
jokes.explanation: Good commentary on military promotion standards and Bart's character

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Despite all negative qualities, the boy could still outrank others in the military.

jokes.text: I'll thank you to stop pinching my Botticelli.
jokes.punchline: Pretentious complaint about handling priceless art
jokes.explanation: Good Burns pretentiousness with art knowledge showing off

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Unhand that painting, you philistine! That's a priceless Rembrandt! Well, actually it's a forgery, but a priceless forgery. Do you have any idea what the oils alone are worth? Of course you don't. Your kind never does.

jokes.text: So long, Sarge. See you at the reunion in November.
jokes.punchline: Cheerful goodbye mentioning reunion Grampa won't attend
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor with Burns' casual attitude toward murder

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Goodbye, Simpson. I'm sure we'll meet again in the afterlife.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I doubt it.

jokes.text: It's been an honor to serve you, sir.
jokes.punchline: Smithers grateful even while being sacrificed
jokes.explanation: Perfect Smithers devotion even in face of abandonment

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, we need to lighten the load. You're going overboard.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Yes, sir. Thank you for the opportunity, sir.

jokes.text: You're out of my unit. You're out of the tontine. And that means the paintings are mine! Private, you are dismissed!
jokes.setup: Grampa confronting Burns about attempted murder
jokes.punchline: Military-style dismissal ending with treasure claim
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with military formality for dramatic confrontation

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, you tried to kill me!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, you're fired.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: You can't fire me, I don't work for you anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Then you're double fired.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, I'm taking the gold!

jokes.text: Ja, ja, ja. Mach schnell mit the art things, huh? I must get back to Dance Central in Stuttgart in time to see Kraftwerk.
jokes.punchline: Modern German stereotypes and electronic music reference
jokes.explanation: Good cultural reference humor mixing German stereotypes with 90s techno culture

dialogue.speaker: Baron
dialogue.line: I am a direct descendant of the great German masters. I have come to reclaim ze artwork zat belongs to my family!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, we're not giving it back. Finders keepers.

dialogue.speaker: Baron
dialogue.line: But you do not understand! In modern Germany, we have evolved. We now listen to Kraftwerk and eat only sausages zat are exactly 6 inches long.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, that's like, the most German thing I've ever heard.

jokes.text: I won't get embarrassed. I don't care who knows I love my grampa.
jokes.setup: Grampa expecting Bart to be embarrassed by affection
jokes.punchline: Bart's public declaration of love
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment showing Bart's growth and genuine affection

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I'm gonna hug you in front of all your friends, and you're gonna be so embarrassed!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Bring it on, old man. I love you, Grandpa!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I love you so much, I'm gonna say it again in front of everybody: I LOVE YOU, GRAMPA!

jokes.text: Hey, fun boys! Get a room!
jokes.setup: Bart and Grampa having tender moment
jokes.punchline: Nelson's inappropriate sexual comment about family affection
jokes.explanation: Classic Nelson inappropriate comment that undermines the sweet moment

jokes.text: Shh!
jokes.punchline: Someone shushing during credits
jokes.explanation: Minor ending gag, minimal impact

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Shhhhhh!

tmdb_overview: When a fellow veteran dies, Grandpa Simpson and Mr. Burns fight for buried WWII treasure as the only surviving members of their battalion.
insight: Abe's boxing comeback carries a thin 73-point episode on character comedy alone.
